content.text: Human grading is the practice of having people — domain experts, trained annotators, or end users — evaluate model outputs and assign quality scores. It is the gold standard for tasks where correctness requires genuine understanding: a human can judge whether a medical summary is accurate, whether a customer service reply is empathetic, or whether a code explanation is clear. No automated grader can reliably do these things without itself being a capable model.

attrs.alt: Human grading workflow: eval cases → task queue → annotator (with rubric) → IAA check → aggregate scores

content.text: Despite this accuracy advantage, human grading is slow and expensive. A thoughtful annotator working at a sustainable pace scores roughly 30–60 examples per hour on moderate-complexity tasks. At scale, this means human grading is used selectively — for calibration datasets, release-gate evals, and production samples — not for every iteration of a prompt.
The decision to use human grading follows from the task, not from preference. Some tasks have no credible automated alternative.
LLM judges may hallucinate medical facts
Aesthetic judgment is subjective and cultural
Surface phrasing does not capture emotional register
Novel attack patterns evade rule-based detectors
Automated graders must be validated against something
No code or LLM judge exists yet for this output type

Human grading is also the reference standard for calibrating LLM judges. Before trusting an LLM-based grader, you must measure its correlation with human scores on a shared dataset. Without human labels as ground truth, there is no way to know whether the automated grader is reliable.
The Three Types of Human Graders

Domain experts have specialized knowledge that makes them the only credible graders for high-stakes professional tasks. A physician grading a clinical summary can detect subtle errors in dosage, contraindications, or diagnostic reasoning that a generalist annotator would miss.
Medical, legal, financial, scientific, or engineering tasks where errors have real-world consequences and require professional judgment.
Professional networks, academic collaborators, or specialized annotation vendors with vetted domain expertise. Expect longer lead times and higher costs.

Even experts need clear rubrics. Without one, two physicians may have different thresholds for what counts as a "correct" summary. Define the evaluation criteria before the expert sees the first example.
Domain experts are expensive, so use them on smaller, strategically selected eval sets — typically held-out release-gate examples or high-risk production samples.

Trained annotators are generalists who can be taught to apply a rubric consistently. They are the most common type of human grader for general-purpose LLM evals: helpfulness, clarity, tone, format correctness, and safety.
The quality of trained annotator work depends almost entirely on the quality of the annotation guidelines and calibration process.
A detailed document covering: what to evaluate, how each scale point is defined, worked examples of each level, common edge cases, and what to do when unsure.
Before live annotation begins, annotators independently score a shared set of examples, then discuss disagreements as a group. This aligns intuitions before they diverge at scale.
Periodically re-run known examples (golden sets) through the annotator pool to detect drift. An annotator whose scores shift over time may be fatiguing or deviating from the rubric.

End users provide implicit quality signal through their behavior: thumbs up/down ratings, session length, retry rates, and conversation abandonment. This is not a replacement for explicit annotation, but it is a real-time, zero-cost signal from the population that actually uses the system.
Direct signal, but biased toward extreme cases. Users rate responses they strongly like or dislike; neutral responses go unrated.
If users frequently regenerate a response or rephrase their question, the first response probably missed. This is a weak but scalable signal.
Short sessions after a model response may indicate the user found what they needed — or gave up. Context matters.
User signals measure engagement and satisfaction, not accuracy. A response that confidently states a falsehood may receive a thumbs up if it sounds plausible.
The annotation interface and task design determine as much of the outcome as annotator quality. A confusing task produces noisy labels even with skilled annotators.

Asking annotators to evaluate five dimensions simultaneously produces lower quality scores on all five than asking them to evaluate one dimension at a time. Structure the task so each rating decision is atomic.

{
  "task_id": "abc123",
  "model_output": "The treatment for type 2 diabetes involves...",
  "question": "Rate the factual accuracy of this response.",
  "scale": "1-5",
  "rubric": {
    "5": "All factual claims are correct and clinically accurate.",
    "4": "One minor inaccuracy that does not affect core advice.",
    "3": "One significant inaccuracy that could mislead a patient.",
    "2": "Multiple inaccuracies; the core claim is questionable.",
    "1": "Fundamentally wrong or potentially harmful."
  },
  "notes_required": true
}
Accuracy, helpfulness, safety, and tone each get their own annotation form. Multi-dimensional forms trade off quality across dimensions.
Asking annotators to write a short note on their score catches two failure modes: scores assigned without reading the response, and scores where the annotator is unsure but the rubric doesn't help.
Annotators who complete tasks suspiciously fast (below a minimum read time for the content length) may be clicking through without reading. Log timestamps.
Write Concrete Rubrics with Anchoring Examples
A rubric without examples leaves too much room for interpretation. Each level should have a worked example that shows what it looks like in practice.

HELPFULNESS RUBRIC — Customer Support Responses

5 — Fully resolves the user's issue, provides correct steps, anticipates follow-up questions.
    Example: "To reset your password: 1) Click 'Forgot password' on login. 2) Enter your email.
    3) Check your inbox for a reset link. If you don't receive it within 5 minutes, check spam."

4 — Resolves the issue with minor omissions (e.g. forgot to mention checking spam).
    Example: "Click 'Forgot password', enter your email, and follow the instructions in the email."

3 — Partially helpful — provides the right direction but leaves the user to figure out details.
    Example: "You can reset your password from the login page."

2 — Tangentially related but does not resolve the issue.
    Example: "Password issues can be caused by many things. Try a different browser."

1 — Not helpful or actively misleading.
    Example: "Passwords are case-sensitive, make sure you're using the right one." (doesn't help reset)

Inter-annotator agreement (IAA) measures how consistently different annotators score the same examples. Low IAA means the rubric is ambiguous, the task is too subjective, or the annotators need more training.

from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
import numpy as np

def inter_annotator_agreement(scores_a: list[int], scores_b: list[int]) -> dict:
    """
    Compute Cohen's kappa for two annotators on the same examples.
    scores_a, scores_b: lists of integer ratings (same length, same examples)
    """
    kappa = cohen_kappa_score(scores_a, scores_b)
    exact_agreement = np.mean(np.array(scores_a) == np.array(scores_b))
    adjacent_agreement = np.mean(np.abs(np.array(scores_a) - np.array(scores_b)) <= 1)
    return {
        "cohen_kappa": round(kappa, 3),
        "exact_agreement": round(exact_agreement, 3),
        "adjacent_agreement": round(adjacent_agreement, 3),
        "n": len(scores_a),
    }

# Example: two annotators scored 20 examples on a 1-5 scale
annotator_a = [5, 4, 3, 5, 2, 4, 4, 3, 5, 4, 3, 2, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 3, 4, 5]
annotator_b = [5, 4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 3, 3, 5, 4, 4, 2, 5, 4, 3, 4, 5, 3, 4, 4]
print(inter_annotator_agreement(annotator_a, annotator_b))
# {'cohen_kappa': 0.89, 'exact_agreement': 0.90, 'adjacent_agreement': 1.0, 'n': 20}
Stop; revise rubric and retrain before proceeding
Major rubric revision needed; review disagreement cases together
Review edge cases; add rubric examples for problematic levels
Acceptable for most evals; spot-check disagreements
Rubric is well-calibrated; scale annotation confidently
Measure IAA on a shared calibration set (20–50 examples) before launching full annotation. If IAA is below 0.60, do not proceed — the labels will be too noisy to be meaningful.
When two annotators disagree by more than one scale point, adjudication resolves the conflict. There are three standard approaches.
Use a third annotator; take the most common score
Binary or ordinal scales with fast turnaround
A senior annotator or domain expert reviews the disagreement and decides
High-stakes tasks where the 'right' answer matters
Take the mean of all annotator scores
Scales where partial credit is meaningful (0.0–1.0)
Scaling Human Grading Without Losing Quality
Human grading does not scale linearly with annotator count. Adding more annotators without infrastructure produces noisy, inconsistent labels.

Use platforms that handle task assignment, time tracking, IAA calculation, and quality dashboards rather than managing spreadsheets manually. This reduces coordination overhead and surfaced systematic errors faster.
Embed known-answer examples (golden items) throughout the annotation queue without annotators knowing which items are golden. Score annotators on these to detect quality drift over time.
When the full eval set is too large for human grading, sample strategically. Oversample edge cases, failure modes, and high-risk categories rather than sampling uniformly.
Annotator accuracy declines after 2–3 hours of continuous judgment tasks. Structure sessions to end before fatigue degrades quality. This matters more than hiring more annotators.
Use code-based or LLM-based graders for the bulk of the eval set; reserve human grading for a calibration subset and for cases where automated graders are uncertain.
The most important use of human grading may not be direct scoring of model outputs. It is the creation of labeled datasets that calibrate and validate automated graders.

import json
from pathlib import Path

def build_calibration_dataset(annotation_file: str, min_iaa: float = 0.70) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Filter human-annotated examples to keep only high-agreement cases.
    These become the calibration set for validating automated graders.
    """
    annotations = [json.loads(line) for line in Path(annotation_file).read_text().splitlines()]

    calibration = []
    for ex in annotations:
        scores = ex.get("annotator_scores", [])
        if len(scores) < 2:
            continue
        # Only keep examples where annotators closely agreed
        if max(scores) - min(scores) <= 1:
            calibration.append({
                "input": ex["input"],
                "output": ex["output"],
                "gold_score": sum(scores) / len(scores),
                "annotator_count": len(scores),
            })

    return calibration

# Result: a clean calibration dataset
# Use it to measure Pearson correlation between your LLM judge and human scores

A calibration dataset of 100–300 high-agreement human-labeled examples is often more valuable than 1000 loosely labeled ones. The calibration set is the reference your automated graders must match; its quality determines how much you can trust the automated graders downstream.
Human graders introduce systematic biases that can corrupt an entire annotation batch if left unchecked. Awareness of these biases is the first step to designing around them.

Annotators who score several high-quality responses in a row may rate subsequent average responses lower than they would in isolation. Mitigation: shuffle examples randomly rather than presenting them in quality order.

A response that is well-formatted and confidently worded tends to receive higher accuracy scores — even when its factual claims are wrong. Mitigation: explicitly instruct annotators to evaluate accuracy independently of presentation. Consider a plain-text rendering for accuracy-focused tasks.
In multi-response comparison tasks, annotators favor responses they read first. Mitigation: randomize the order of responses across annotators. Use within-annotator counterbalancing.
Accuracy drops after extended annotation sessions. Studies of professional annotators show meaningful quality degradation after 90–120 minutes of continuous rating tasks.

def flag_fatigue_risk(annotation_log: list[dict], session_limit_minutes: int = 90) -> list[str]:
    """
    Return annotator IDs whose continuous session exceeded the limit.
    annotation_log: list of {annotator_id, task_id, start_time, end_time} dicts.
    """
    from collections import defaultdict

    sessions = defaultdict(list)
    for entry in annotation_log:
        sessions[entry["annotator_id"]].append(entry)

    at_risk = []
    for annotator_id, tasks in sessions.items():
        tasks_sorted = sorted(tasks, key=lambda t: t["start_time"])
        session_start = tasks_sorted[0]["start_time"]
        for prev, curr in zip(tasks_sorted, tasks_sorted[1:]):
            gap_sec = (curr["start_time"] - prev["end_time"]).seconds
            if gap_sec > 300:  # 5-min break resets the session
                session_start = curr["start_time"]
            elapsed_min = (curr["end_time"] - session_start).seconds / 60
            if elapsed_min > session_limit_minutes:
                at_risk.append(annotator_id)
                break
    return list(set(at_risk))
Human annotation budgets are always limited. Allocating them well is a practical skill.
High IAA needed; these drive trust in all automated evals
Quality over quantity; these examples block or allow shipping
Trend detection; small sample is sufficient for drift
Requires specialist skills and high failure-mode coverage

Budget allocation depends on the stakes: a medical advice system justifies expert annotators for 500+ examples; a recipe generator does not. Match annotation investment to the cost of a quality failure in that domain.
Human Grading in Practice: A Minimal Setup
Teams starting out often lack annotation platforms and dedicated annotators. A minimal human grading setup that still produces reliable data:
Even for internal use, define each scale level before grading begins. Changing the rubric after annotation starts invalidates earlier labels.
Have all graders independently score the same 20 examples before starting independent work. Discuss all disagreements.
Give each grader a manageable batch (50–100 examples). Stagger timing so calibration alignment stays fresh.
Randomly assign 10% of examples to two graders. Use these to compute IAA and flag drift over time.
Weekly, review the lowest-scoring examples. Confirm they are genuine failures, not annotation errors. Add the clearest ones to the permanent eval set.

The minimal setup above produces usable human eval data with two graders and a spreadsheet. It is not optimal, but it is far better than no human grading at all — and it creates the rubric, calibration set, and IAA process that scales when resources grow.

Human grading is the ground-truth reference for LLM evaluation. It is required for tasks that demand genuine domain expertise, and it is the source of calibration data that validates every automated grader. The workflow has three core components: a clear rubric with worked examples at each level, an inter-annotator agreement measurement before full annotation begins, and an adjudication process for disagreements. Human grading does not scale through volume alone — it scales through better rubrics, shorter annotation sessions, golden-set quality monitoring, and strategic pairing with automated graders. Used correctly, a relatively small human-graded dataset provides the foundation that makes all automated evaluation trustworthy.
